title: The Convergent Core: Connecting Seed Stability, Cross-Model Universality, and Causal Importance of Sparse Autoencoder Features
description: We identify a 'convergent core' of sparse autoencoder features that are reliably discovered across random seeds and show that these seed-stable features are also more universal across different model architectures and more causally important for model behavior, providing a principled, cost-free method for distinguishing genuine model features from optimization artifacts.
motivation: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are the dominant tool for mechanistic interpretability, yet recent work shows that SAEs trained with different random seeds learn substantially different features (only ~30% overlap). Simultaneously, other work shows SAE feature spaces are surprisingly universal across entirely different models. This paradox—features are unreliable across seeds but reliable across models—has not been studied, leaving practitioners without principled guidance on which SAE features to trust. Resolving this tension would benefit all downstream applications of SAEs including circuit discovery, model steering, and safety auditing.
proposed_approach: We train TopK SAEs with 5 random seeds on GPT-2 Small, Pythia-160M, and Pythia-410M, then compute a 'convergence score' for each feature based on pairwise decoder cosine similarity across seeds. We measure cross-model universality via activation-correlation-based feature matching, and causal importance via zero-ablation KL divergence. We test whether convergence score predicts both universality and causal importance, characterize the geometric and semantic properties of core vs. peripheral features, and analyze whether the variable periphery collectively spans a consistent complementary subspace across seeds.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Trained on the Same Data Learn Different Features
related_work.relation: Established that SAEs with different seeds learn only ~30% shared features. We build on this finding by asking WHICH features are shared and whether they are more genuine.

related_work.title: Stable and Steerable Sparse Autoencoders with Weight Regularization
related_work.relation: Proposed L2 regularization to improve seed stability. They focus on improving training; we focus on exploiting natural variation as a diagnostic signal.

related_work.title: Enhancing Neural Network Interpretability with Feature-Aligned Sparse Autoencoders
related_work.authors: Luke Marks, Alasdair Paren, David Krueger, Fazl Barez
related_work.relation: Proposed Mutual Feature Regularization to train SAEs jointly for shared features. They modify training; we analyze independently trained SAEs post-hoc.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Reveal Universal Feature Spaces Across Large Language Models
related_work.authors: Michael Lan, Philip Torr, Austin Meek, Ashkan Khakzar, David Krueger, Fazl Barez
related_work.relation: Showed SAE feature spaces are universal across models. We test whether the universal features are the same as the seed-stable features.

related_work.title: Universal Sparse Autoencoders: Interpretable Cross-Model Concept Alignment
related_work.authors: Harrish Thasarathan, Julian Forsyth, Thomas Fel, Matthew Kowal, Konstantinos G. Derpanis
related_work.relation: Introduced Universal SAEs for cross-model concept alignment in vision models. We study universality in language models and connect it to seed stability.

related_work.title: Scaling and Evaluating Sparse Autoencoders
related_work.authors: Leo Gao, Tom Dupre la Tour, Henk Tillman, Gabriel Goh, Rajan Troll, Alec Radford, Ilya Sutskever, Jan Leike, Jeffrey Wu
related_work.relation: Introduced TopK SAEs and scaling laws. We use TopK SAEs as our primary architecture and build on their evaluation methodology.

related_work.title: SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders in Language Model Interpretability
related_work.authors: Adam Karvonen, Can Rager, Johnny Lin, et al.
related_work.relation: Provides comprehensive SAE evaluation metrics. Our convergence score offers a complementary, training-free quality signal.

related_work.title: A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders
related_work.authors: David Chanin, James Wilken-Smith, Tomas Dulka, Hardik Bhatnagar, Satvik Golechha, Joseph Bloom
related_work.relation: Identified feature splitting and absorption as failure modes. Seed-variable features may include split/absorbed variants of core features.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models
related_work.authors: Hoagy Cunningham, Aidan Ewart, Logan Riggs, Robert Huben, Lee Sharkey
related_work.relation: Foundational work on SAEs for LLM interpretability. We build on their methodology for training and evaluating SAEs.

related_work.title: Automatically Interpreting Millions of Features in Large Language Models
related_work.authors: Goncalo Paulo, Alex Mallen, Caden Juang, Nora Belrose
related_work.relation: Developed automated interpretability scoring pipeline. We use their methods for supplementary evaluation of feature quality.

hypothesis: Sparse autoencoder features that are reliably discovered across random seeds (seed-stable) are also more universal across different model architectures and more causally important for model behavior than seed-variable features. Seed convergence score serves as a cheap, training-free proxy for feature quality.
success_criteria: Statistically significant positive correlation (Spearman rho > 0.2, p < 0.01) between seed convergence score and cross-model universality score
Seed-stable (core) features have significantly higher mean causal importance (zero-ablation KL divergence) than seed-variable (peripheral) features, with Cohen's d > 0.3
Convergence score outperforms random partition baseline as a feature quality predictor (higher AUC-ROC) on at least 2 of 3 models
Secondary: convergence score correlates with automated interpretability scores
Secondary: peripheral subspaces show above-chance consistency across seeds (principal angles smaller than random baseline)
